data.title: A Comprehensive Guide to GPT-5.5: Starting Today, OpenAI Will No Longer Sell Tokens
data.description:
Author: Li Hailun, Tencent Technology

On April 23 local time, OpenAI officially released its new generation flagship model GPT-5.5, which it positions as "a new level of intelligence for real-world work," and is also an important step towards a new way of computing.

84.9% of tasks reach professional level

Figure: Comparison of GPT-5.5 with competing products in core benchmark tests such as Terminal-Bench 2.0, GDPval, and OSWorld-Verified

First, let's look at the performance of the evaluation model in real professional scenarios.

OpenAI used a benchmark test called "GDPval," which requires models to complete a full set of professional tasks. The test covers 44 professional scenarios, including financial modeling, legal analysis, data science reporting, operational planning, and more. The results show that GPT-5.5 reached or exceeded the level of industry professionals in 84.9% of the tasks. In comparison, GPT-5.4 scored 83.0%, Claude Opus 4.7 80.3%, and Gemini 3.1 Pro only 67.3%. This gap isn't limited to the overall score. In the spreadsheet modeling task, GPT-5.5 achieved 88.5% in internal testing; it also outperformed its predecessor in investment banking-level modeling tasks. Early testers' feedback was consistent: GPT-5.5 Pro's responses showed significant improvements over GPT-5.4 Pro in terms of comprehensiveness, structure, and practicality, particularly in the business, legal, education, and data science fields. Looking at numbers alone can be monotonous, so OpenAI went all out this time, revealing its own workstations. OpenAI stated that over 85% of its employees use Codex weekly, covering multiple departments including finance, communications, marketing, product, and data science. The communications team used it to analyze six months of speech invitation data, building an automated grading process; the finance team used it to review 24,771 K-1 tax forms, totaling 71,637 pages, completing the task two weeks ahead of schedule compared to last year; and the marketing team saved 5 to 10 hours per person per week by automating weekly report generation. This isn't just a lab demo; it's become part of daily work. OpenAI claims that GPT-5.5 is currently its most powerful autonomous programming model. On Terminal-Bench 2.0 (testing complex command-line workflows requiring planning, iteration, and tool coordination), GPT-5.5 scored 82.7%, an improvement of nearly 8 percentage points compared to GPT-5.4's 75.1%, while consuming fewer tokens. On SWE-Bench Pro (evaluating the ability to solve real-world GitHub problems on the first try), GPT-5.5 scored 58.6%. In the internal Expert-SWE benchmark (a long-cycle programming task with a median human completion time of approximately 20 hours), GPT-5.5 also outperformed GPT-5.4.

Figure: Scatter plot of Terminal-Bench 2.0 and Expert-SWE

Driven by GPT-5.5, Codex can now independently complete the entire development process from code generation, functional testing to visual debugging, starting from a single prompt.

The official OpenAI demonstration cases show that the space mission application is built based on real NASA orbital data, supports 3D interactive control, and achieves realistic physical accuracy in orbital mechanics simulation; the earthquake tracker connects to real-time data sources and completes visualization, demonstrating that the model has the complete ability to call external APIs, process dynamic data, and render in real time. Regarding user feedback, Every founder and CEO Dan Shipper shared an experience: He previously encountered a bug after deployment, which he couldn't fix himself for several days, and finally had to ask the company's best engineer to rewrite part of the system. After GPT-5.5 was released, he conducted an experiment—putting the model back to the state before the bug was fixed, to see if it could automatically arrive at the same solution as the engineer. GPT-5.4 couldn't do it, but GPT-5.5 could. He commented: "This is the first programming model I've used that truly has conceptual clarity."

An NVIDIA engineer's comment was even more blunt: "Losing access to GPT-5.5 feels like amputation."

Cursor co-founder and CEO Michael Truell added: GPT-5.5 is smarter and more resilient than GPT-5.4, able to persist longer in complex, long-duration tasks without prematurely stopping—which is exactly what engineering work needs most. Knowledge Work: For the First Time, AI Can Truly "Use" a Computer. In the OSWorld-Verified test (which tests whether a model can independently operate a real computer environment), GPT-5.5 achieved a success rate of 78.7%, higher than GPT-5.4's 75.0% and also better than Claude Opus 4.7's 78.0%. This isn't just screenshot analysis; it's real screen control: seeing the interface, clicking, inputting, switching between multiple tools, until the task is completed. GPT-5.5 gives people the first experience that AI can truly share the same computer with you. In the Tau2-bench benchmark for telecom customer service workflows, GPT-5.5 achieved an accuracy of 98.0% without prompt word optimization, while GPT-5.4 only achieved 92.8%. This means the model has a sufficiently deep understanding of the task intent to handle complex multi-step dialogue processes without the need for carefully designed prompt words. Regarding tool search capabilities, GPT-5.5 scored 84.4% in the BrowseComp test, while GPT-5.5 Pro achieved an even higher 90.1%, indicating that in research-type tasks requiring comprehensive reasoning across multiple information sources, the model demonstrated a strong ability for continuous retrieval and information integration. Scientific Research: Assisting in the Discovery of New Mathematical Proofs In this release, GPT-5.5's performance in the scientific research field is perhaps the most unexpected aspect. In the past, when we talked about AI in scientific research, it was more of an "auxiliary tool" used for literature review, coding, and data processing. But this time, its role has clearly shifted forward, participating in more core aspects: complex reasoning, and even the discovery itself. On GeneBench (a multi-stage data analysis benchmark in genetics and quantitative biology), GPT-5.5 scored 25.0%, while GPT-5.4 scored 19.0%. These tasks typically correspond to several days' worth of work for scientific experts. The models need to reason about potentially erroneous data, handle hidden confounding factors, and correctly implement modern statistical methods under almost no supervision. As can be seen from the graph, as the number of output tokens increases, GPT-5.5 consistently outperforms GPT-5.4 in terms of score improvement, with a significant gap appearing around 15,000 tokens—meaning that for long tasks requiring deep reasoning, GPT-5.5's advantage further amplifies with increasing task complexity. On BixBench (a real-world benchmark for bioinformatics and data analytics), GPT-5.5 outperformed GPT-5.4's 74.0% with a score of 80.5%, ranking among the top-scoring models. What truly garnered attention was a specific case: an internal version of GPT-5.5 equipped with a custom tool framework helped discover a new mathematical proof about Ramsey numbers, which was then verified in the formal proof tool Lean. Ramsey numbers are a core research object in combinatorics, and achievements in this field are extremely rare and technically challenging. This wasn't AI providing code or explanations, but a genuine contribution of a mathematical proof. Its practical applications are equally compelling. Derya Unutmaz, professor of immunology at Jackson Lab, used GPT-5.5 Pro to analyze a gene expression dataset containing 62 samples and nearly 28,000 genes, generating a detailed research report and extracting key findings and research questions—a task he said typically takes teams several months to complete. Bartosz Naskręcki, assistant professor of mathematics at Poznan Adam Mickiewicz University, built an algebraic geometry application in 11 minutes using GPT-5.5 in Codex, based solely on a single prompt. This application visualized the intersection of two quadratic surfaces and transformed the resulting curve into a Weierstrass model. The equation coefficients displayed in real-time on the right can be directly used for subsequent mathematical research; the entire process, from the prompt to a working research tool, was completed independently by the model. Image: Screenshot of an algebraic geometry application built by Professor Bartosz Naskręcki—visualization of quadric surface intersections and real-time calculation interface for the Weierstrass equation. Axiom Bio co-founder Brandon White's assessment is even more direct: "If OpenAI maintains this momentum, the foundation of drug discovery will change by the end of the year." Inference Efficiency: AI Optimizes Its Infrastructure for the First Time This release has an easily overlooked detail, but it may be the most noteworthy technical advancement. GPT-5.5 is a larger and more powerful model, yet its single-token latency in real-world service is on par with GPT-5.4. To maintain the same latency with greater capabilities, OpenAI redesigned the inference system as a whole—and Codex and GPT-5.5 themselves were directly involved in the optimization process. This is visually apparent from the Artificial Analysis intelligence index graph: the horizontal axis represents the total output tokens (logarithmic scale), and the vertical axis represents the overall intelligence score. GPT-5.5's curve not only outperforms GPT-5.4, Claude Opus 4.7, and Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview in all aspects of scoring, but more importantly, it achieves the same score level that other models need to consume more tokens to reach in the range where token consumption is low—stronger capabilities at lower costs, which is a direct manifestation of "efficiency improvement".

Figure: Artificial Analysis Smart Index Line Chart

Specifically, the team faced the problem of load balancing: previously, requests were split into a fixed number of blocks to balance the workload on the GPUs, but static block splitting was not optimal for all traffic patterns. Codex analyzed several weeks of production traffic data and wrote a custom heuristic algorithm, which improved token generation speed by more than 20%. GPT-5.5 is co-designed, co-trained, and co-deployed with NVIDIA GB200 and GB300 NVL72 systems. In other words, this generation of models participates in optimizing the inference architecture of the service itself—this is not a metaphor, but a literal statement that "AI improves the system running itself." Cybersecurity: Enhanced Capabilities, Tighter Controls GPT-5.5 shows a clear improvement in cybersecurity capabilities. In CyberGym testing, GPT-5.5 scored 81.8%, GPT-5.4 scored 79.0%, and Claude Opus 4.7 scored 73.1%. In the internal Capture the Flag (CTF) challenge, GPT-5.5 scored 88.1%, and GPT-5.4 scored 83.7%. OpenAI has rated GPT-5.5's cybersecurity and biological/chemical capabilities as "High" under the Contingency Preparedness Framework, not yet reaching the "Critical" level, but representing a clear improvement over its predecessor. At the same time, it acknowledges that the newly deployed, more stringent risk classifier "may initially be inconvenient for some users" and will continue to be adjusted. To balance defense needs with access restrictions, OpenAI launched the "Trusted Access to Cybersecurity" program: eligible security researchers and critical infrastructure defenders can apply for more lenient access permissions to use advanced cybersecurity capabilities with less friction. The logic behind this is that capability proliferation is an irreversible trend, and a more realistic path than restricting proliferation is to allow defenders to use the most powerful tools before attackers.

data.title: DeFi's core strengths are destroying themselves.
data.description:
Author: Thejaswini, Source: Buttercup Network, Translated by: Shaw Jinse Finance

“Death” is a very heavy word. As I've always maintained, I'm optimistic about DeFi. But then again, every few months, DeFi repeats the same risky farce, then pretends to be caught off guard by the outcome. Over time, one has to admire this persistence.

On April 18th, attackers exploited a vulnerability in Kelp DAO's LayerZero cross-chain bridge to steal 116,500 rsETH, worth approximately $292 million, representing about 18% of the token's total circulating supply. The attackers didn't immediately sell them off—hackers are now more cautious. They deposited 89,567 of the stolen tokens into Aave V3 as collateral, using this to borrow native ETH, and then made their escape. Thus, Aave incurred approximately $196 million in bad debt, and these tokens used as collateral should not have appeared out of thin air just hours earlier. Within 48 hours, Aave's total value locked (TVL) plummeted from $26.4 billion to nearly $20 billion, and has now fallen to $14 billion; its native token, AAVE, has also dropped by 16%. SparkLend and Fluid have frozen the rsETH-related lending market, Lido has suspended its earnETH product, and Ethena, even without direct risk exposure, has urgently shut down all its LayerZero cross-chain bridges. Like a neighbor's house on fire, an uncle, fearing the spread, throws all his furniture out the window. In the current industry climate, how can I criticize such self-preservation? Aave founder Stani Kulechov clarified on Twitter that the Aave contract itself was not compromised, the emergency pause mechanism functioned correctly, and the entire system operated exactly as designed. If this system truly operates as designed, then the design itself is a disaster. Kelp's cross-chain bridges adopt a single-node decentralized verification network (1-of-1 DVN) architecture. Below is an explanation of its specific meaning: Single Validator: Only one entity is responsible for verifying transactions. Single Signature Authorization: Asset transfers can be approved with just a digital "confirmation signature." Unlimited Authority: With just this one signature, one has the ultimate authority to mint hundreds of millions of dollars worth of new tokens. Traditional banks, even for a simple $10,000 wire transfer, typically require approval and signatures from two to three managers. However, the Kelp protocol's cross-chain bridge, which handles billions of dollars in cross-chain asset transfers, prioritizes low cost and a "simple" architecture, completely lacking effective checks and balances and risk control mechanisms. Next, let's talk about Aave. Aave previously accepted rsETH as collateral, with a loan-to-value (LTV) ratio as high as 93%. Simply put, for every $1 worth of rsETH a user deposits, Aave lends out $0.93 of real assets. Only with extremely high confidence in the collateral would such aggressive parameters be set. However, the total circulating supply of this token, which Aave trusts, can increase by 18% with just one transaction. The deposit limit for this token is extremely lenient; a single wallet can deposit 90,000 rsETH at once without triggering any risk control warnings. How can such risk parameters be described? Perhaps the answer is self-evident. Every post-mortem review yields the same conclusion: the smart contract executed flawlessly, the oracle data reporting was normal, the liquidation mechanism was triggered according to the rules, and the emergency pause function was successfully activated within minutes. These are all facts, but none of them grasp the core of the problem. Aave's protocol design is fundamentally flawed: as long as the risk hasn't been publicly exposed, the system allows anonymous wallets to deposit hundreds of millions of dollars in collateralized assets without underlying backing, and then issue real lending debt based on this. This design stems from a fundamental belief: as long as assets have oracle pricing and the deposit limit is sufficiently generous, they can be used unconditionally as collateral for lending. Those who believe in "code is law" often take this as reassurance. However, "code is law" doesn't mean the rules are reasonable; it only means the rules will be mechanically enforced without any room for maneuver. If the rules state, "Any token with oracle pricing can be used for high-value collateralized lending," then this unconditional enforcement is the most frightening aspect. For the past six years, DeFi has been touting composability. Everyone's talking about "financial Lego": nested protocols, free flow of funds, and highly efficient and sophisticated mechanisms. In contrast, traditional banks would never readily accept interbank IOUs as collateral; they always have dedicated risk control teams conducting thorough reviews. This is a lesson learned from the 2008 financial crisis: highly interconnected financial positions only lead to shared risk, not shared returns. Globally, all financial regulatory agencies saw the fatal flaws of DeFi architecture as early as 2019. Layered counterparty risk, zero-discount risk control, no limits on transaction amounts, and a lack of a lender of last resort—yet the entire industry blindly believed that on-chain transparency could replace all risk control measures. Aave's core liquidity pool utilization once reached 100%, preventing depositors from redeeming their Ethereum. In traditional finance, this would be a classic bank run. When users deposit assets into Aave, they are essentially endorsing four layers of risk: Aave's risk control parameters, the rsETH oracle, the Kelp cross-chain bridge, and Kelp's decentralized verification network configuration. The last one is something most Aave users have never even heard of. The Cost Bill: Kelp incident losses: $292 million; Drift incident losses three weeks ago: $285 million; In just 21 days, these two industry-leading protocols have collectively lost nearly $500 million. In the first quarter of 2026, the number of crypto venture capital deals plummeted by 48.9% year-on-year, and the number of funding rounds shrank from 358 to 183. During the same quarter, AI companies raised a staggering $242 billion, accounting for approximately 80% of the total global venture capital investment. Capital that was initially intended to improve the underlying infrastructure of DeFi is now flowing into other more speculative and attractive areas. The innovations DeFi truly desperately needs—a conservative and prudent loan-to-value ratio, a multi-validator cross-chain bridge architecture, a robust insurance guarantee mechanism, and routine audits of basic, standard configurations—have been ignored. No one has ever become rich by considering the potential disasters of a single validator node (DVN) becoming a single point of failure. Of course, the hackers of the April 18th attack were the sole exception. We can do some simple calculations: On April 18th, Aave's security reserve pool held $390 million, while the platform's outstanding loan balance reached a staggering $17.82 billion. This implied capital adequacy ratio was only 2.2%. Basel III requires banks to have a minimum capital adequacy ratio of 8%, with JPMorgan Chase actually meeting the requirement at 15.5%. Aave's staking penalty cap for AAVE is 30%, meaning the maximum usable funds in this safety net are only about $180 million, further reducing the effective capital adequacy ratio to around 1.0%. A simple and feasible solution has always existed: **adopting a multi-validator (Multi-DVN) architecture, lowering the loan-to-value ratio for assets with questionable creditworthiness, and setting a truly effective cap on token supply.** The most costly solution, however, is to acknowledge that **composability is never an advantageous feature; it's merely a beautifully packaged risk transmission mechanism.** I don't believe this is simply a security issue. For six years, DeFi has been trapped in a misconception: equating on-chain transparency with asset security. And every 18 months, this cognitive fallacy is repeatedly confirmed by larger-scale incidents. "Financial Lego" sounds wonderful. But it accurately describes the current reality: stacking plastic blocks on a burning table. This is a lesson I learned from my elders, and it's incredibly insightful.

data.title: DeepSeek V4 Preview: Entering an Era of Millions of Contextual Accessible Resources
data.description:
Source: DeepSeek Official WeChat Account

Today, the preview version of DeepSeek's new model series, DeepSeek-V4, was officially launched and simultaneously open-sourced.

DeepSeek-V4 boasts a million-word ultra-long context, achieving leading performance in both domestic and open-source fields in Agent capabilities, world knowledge, and inference capabilities.

Source: DeepSeek Official WeChat Account

Today, the preview version of DeepSeek-V4, a brand-new model series from DeepSeek, was officially launched and simultaneously open-sourced.

DeepSeek-V4 features a million-word ultra-long context, achieving leading performance in Agent capabilities, world knowledge, and inference performance both domestically and in the open-source field.

The model is available in two versions based on size:

Starting today, log in to the official website  chat.deepseek.com  or the official App to interact with the latest DeepSeek-V4 and explore the new experience of 1M ultra-long context memory. The API service has been updated accordingly; you can call it by changing model_name to deepseek-v4-pro or deepseek-v4-flash.

...

DeepSeek-V4-Pro: Performance Comparable to Top Closed-Source Models

  • Significantly Improved Agent Capabilities: Compared to its predecessor, DeepSeek-V4-Pro's agent capabilities are significantly enhanced. In Agentic Coding benchmarks, V4-Pro has reached the best performance among current open-source models and also performs excellently in other agent-related benchmarks. Currently, DeepSeek-V4 has become the agentic coding model used by company employees. According to evaluation feedback, its user experience is superior to Sonnet 4.5, and its delivery quality is close to Opus 4.6's non-thinking mode, but it still lags behind Opus 4.6's thinking mode. Rich World Knowledge: In world knowledge evaluations, DeepSeek-V4-Pro significantly outperforms other open-source models, only slightly lagging behind the top closed-source model Gemini-Pro-3.1. World-Class Inference Performance: In evaluations of mathematics, STEM, and competition-style code, DeepSeek-V4-Pro surpasses all currently publicly evaluated open-source models, achieving excellent results comparable to the world's top closed-source models.

DeepSeek-V4-Flash: A Faster, More Efficient, and Economical Choice

  • Compared to DeepSeek-V4-Pro, DeepSeek-V4-Flash is slightly inferior in terms of world knowledge reserves, but it demonstrates similar inference capabilities. Due to its smaller model parameters and activations, V4-Flash offers a faster and more economical API service.

DeepSeek-V4-Flash: A Faster, More Efficient, and Economical Choice

  • Compared to DeepSeek-V4-Pro, DeepSeek-V4-Flash is slightly inferior in terms of world knowledge reserves, but it demonstrates similar inference capabilities. Furthermore, due to its smaller model parameters and activations, V4-Flash provides a faster and more economical API service.

Computational cost and memory capacity of DeepSeek-V4 and DeepSeek-V3.2 vary with context length

Agent-specific optimization

DeepSeek-V4 for Claude Code ... It has been adapted and optimized for mainstream Agent products such as OpenClaw, OpenCode, and CodeBuddy, resulting in improved performance in code tasks and document generation tasks. The image below shows an example of a PPT slide generated by V4-Pro within a certain Agent framework:

API Access

Currently, the DeepSeek API has been simultaneously launched on V4-Pro and V4-Flash, supporting OpenAI.

The ChatCompletions and Anthropic interfaces. When accessing a new model, the base_url remains the same, but the model parameter needs to be changed to deepseek-v4-pro or deepseek-v4-flash. The maximum context size for both V4-Pro and V4-Flash is 1MB. Both support both non-thinking mode and thinking mode. The thinking mode allows setting the thinking intensity (high/max) using the `reasoning_effort` parameter. For complex Agent scenarios, it is recommended to use thinking mode and set the intensity to max. For model invocation and parameter adjustment methods, please refer to the API documentation: https://api-docs.deepseek.com/zh-cn/guides/thinking_mode. Please note: The two model names deepseek-chat and deepseek-reasoner in the old API will be discontinued in three months (July 24, 2026). At this stage, these two model names refer to the non-thinking mode and thinking mode of deepseek-v4-flash, respectively.

Open source weighting and local deployment

  • DeepSeek-V4 Model open source link:

https://huggingface.co/collections/deepseek-ai/deepseek-v4

https://modelscope.cn/collections/deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V4

data.title: Tesla Records $173M Loss From Its Bitcoin Investment After BTC Falls To $78,000
data.description:
Tesla Loses Millions In Bitcoin, But Keeps Its Long-Term Bitcoin Position Intact

Tesla has reported a $173 million loss from its bitcoin investment as of the first quarter of 2026. Yet the company keeps its crypto strategy unchanged....

Quick take: Tesla ate a $173M after tax impairment loss (accounting hit from BTC price drop $114k to $88k) on digital assets.

Zero sales. Holdings steady at 11,509 BTC.

SpaceX (Elon led) bags 8,285 BTC too. Signals corporate diamond hands: BTC as reserve asset > short term… pic.twitter.com/Zk1dGkJIIB

— JAVLIS - Your Trading #AIAgent (@javliscom) April 23, 2026

The automaker confirmed it continues to hold 11,509 BTC, signaling an unchanged long-term strategy despite renewed volatility triggered by the rising geopolitical unheavel in the middle east.

However, the reported loss only showed a flawed side of the story, stemming from the accounting requirements that force companies to only record losses without allowing immediate recognition of potential gains in cases of price increase.

This asymmetry means Tesla’s financial results can show significant downside during market dips, while failing to capture potential upside as prices rebound—creating a disconnect between reported earnings and actual exposure.

Despite the paper loss, Tesla’s unchanged holdings reinforce its position among a small group of public companies maintaining Bitcoin as a treasury asset. The company’s exposure, valued in the hundreds of millions of dollars, continues to fluctuate with market conditions but reflects a strategy focused on long-term participation rather than short-term trading.

🚨 STRATEGY OVERTAKES BLACKROCK AS LARGEST BITCOIN HOLDER

Saylor’s Strategy has officially surpassed BlackRock’s IBIT ETF, now holding ~815,061 BTC vs ~802,824 BTC for $IBIT.

The flip comes after its latest buy of 34,164 $BTC for ~$2.54B last week, its third-largest purchase… pic.twitter.com/Iy828mJYAv

— Coin Bureau (@coinbureau) April 21, 2026

The approach mirrors a broader trend among crypto-aligned corporates that absorb interim volatility while maintaining conviction in Bitcoin’s role as a store of value.

Corporate Crypto Strategies Face Structural Constraints

Tesla’s latest results highlight a persistent challenge for companies holding digital assets: accounting frameworks have struggled to keep pace with the characteristics of cryptocurrencies.

Purpose-built for autonomy

Cybercab in production now at Giga Texas pic.twitter.com/Y9qG3KyWBa

— Tesla (@Tesla) April 23, 2026

The current treatment can amplify earnings volatility and obscure underlying performance, particularly during periods of sharp price movement. As more firms consider adding Bitcoin to their balance sheets, the limitations of these standards are becoming increasingly visible.

For Tesla, the $173 million loss underscores how market swings—and the rules governing their reporting—can shape financial outcomes without altering the company’s actual position in crypto.

data.title: Intel's Q1 earnings report delivered a resounding slap in the face to Wall Street.
data.description:
Author: xiaopi, Wall Street Insights

After the market closed on April 23, Intel's stock price rose 20%, approaching $80, a new high since the dot-com bubble.

But this increase itself is actually more worthy of consideration than the financial report figures. Prior to this, among the 34 analysts on Wall Street who tracked Intel, 24 gave it a "Hold" rating, with an average target price of $55.33.

... At that time, Intel's stock price was already at 66—meaning that most institutions' judgments were not just conservative, but had fallen behind reality. A quarterly report that significantly exceeded expectations not only boosted the stock price but also exposed this discrepancy. This 20% after-hours surge was, to some extent, the market completing a belated pricing correction for the institutions. Exceeding Expectations Across the Board: Revenue 13.6 billion, analysts expected 12.4 billion, exceeding expectations by approximately 9.3%. Adjusted EPS 0.29, expected 0.01. Q2 revenue guidance midpoint 14.3 billion, expected 13.1 billion. All three core indicators significantly exceeded expectations, and the exceedance was not marginal but systemic—the implied volatility bet in the options market before the release was 9.3%, but it actually increased by more than two times, meaning that even hedging institutions were caught off guard. The net loss under GAAP was 3.7 billion, and the difference of approximately 4 billion between non-GAAP and GAAP figures came from stock-based compensation, depreciation, and restructuring expenses. These are the real costs Intel is incurring in its foundry transformation, not accounting noise, but also not new information to the market—analysts have already factored them in. What are the 24 Hold institutions waiting for? Behind the Hold consensus, it's not primarily a valuation issue. In the past two years, as Intel has continuously lost its process technology leadership and its market share has been eroded by AMD, Wall Street's deepest concern about the company has been its execution: Lip-Bu Tan's story sounds good, but Intel has said "this time it really will be good" too many times. Therefore, even if the demand logic for AI agents is clear and the story of the CPU's value revaluation at the inference layer holds true, most institutions are still waiting for concrete proof, rather than betting on narratives. This financial report provides precisely that proof—in the form of contracts. Intel announced it will manufacture chips for Elon Musk's Terafab factory, with customers including SpaceX, xAI, and Tesla; on the same day, it signed a multi-year contract with Google, under which Xeon CPUs will provide computing power for AI inference and other workloads on Google Cloud. The timing of these two contracts, announced on the same day as the quarterly report release, is not a coincidence, but rather management's way of telling the market in black and white: "Our demand isn't just a trend prediction; it's already secured revenue." The confidence in the Q2 guidance also stems from this—$13.8 billion to $14.8 billion, the midpoint of which is $1.2 billion higher than the consensus, representing a commitment to existing orders. The logic of data centers has changed. Data center and AI business revenue reached $5.1 billion, a year-over-year increase of 22%, with an operating profit margin of 31%. Before 2023, this division was considered the remnant of NVIDIA's dominance, but the large-scale deployment of AI agent architectures has changed the demand structure. Large model training uses GPUs, but inference—especially enterprise-oriented, latency-sensitive, medium-sized inference workloads—has seen a significant underestimation of CPU demand. As cloud vendors face fierce competition for GPU resources, they have begun to procure server CPUs in large quantities to handle these tasks, and Intel's Xeon is currently the most reliable option in terms of supply. Lip-Bu Tan mentioned in the conference call that several customers are "actively evaluating" the next-generation 18A process technology. The weight of this wording—not just "interested," but "actively evaluating"—indicates that the commercialization of the foundry business is moving from the intention stage to the substantive stage. In contrast, client computing revenue reached $7.7 billion, a year-over-year increase of 1%. The concept of "AI PCs" has been discussed for two years, and Intel has released the Core Ultra series, but this replacement cycle has not yet brought about a real demand surge. This business segment contributes more than half of the company's revenue, yet it has seen almost no growth—making it the least attractive part of this financial report and one of the most real risks. Once the AI ​​demand cycle in data centers fluctuates, CCG has almost no buffering capacity. Foundry Services: How Long Is the Road Ahead? Intel Foundry Services revenue was $5.4 billion, up $165.4 billion year-over-year and $162.4 billion year-over-year, only narrowing by $72 million compared to the previous quarter. This rate of narrowing is usually described as "progress in line with expectations" in investment bank analyst reports, but if you calculate it—at this pace, it will take years, not quarters, for IFS to reach break-even. One of the core reasons BNP Paribas upgraded its rating before the earnings report was a more optimistic data assessment of the 14A node (the successor to 18A)—if 18A mass production goes smoothly, the customer appeal of 14A will be further enhanced. However, every milestone on this path is a reason for holding institutions to wait and see, and something that true bulls must continuously verify. The ensuing chain reaction: The 24 holding institutions are now in a somewhat awkward position. The average target price is 55.33, the stock price touched 55.33 in after-hours trading, and the stock price touched 80 in after-hours trading. This gap can no longer be maintained by "remaining cautious"—when the stock price exceeds the target price by more than 40%, analysts either revise their judgment or admit that they have missed the boat. Historically, whenever such concentrated institutional lag pricing occurs, subsequent batch target price increases themselves act as a boost to the market: each new research report release becomes a bullish signal reported by the media. HSBC has already given a street-high target price of $95 before its earnings report; if more institutions follow suit with upward adjustments, this figure may become a new market anchor. Two developments deserve continued monitoring. First, the pace of the 18A process from "active evaluation" to "contract signing" and then to "mass production"—every specific development on this timeline will directly affect the speed at which IFS's losses narrow and will also affect Intel's foundry attractiveness to external customers. Secondly, the repositioning of those 24 holding institutions is not because their judgment determines the stock price trend, but because their collective adjustment provides an observable signal—when institutional consensus shifts from "primarily holding" to "primarily buying," it usually means that the market's perception of a company has undergone a fundamental change. This financial report doesn't change Intel's quarterly performance, but rather the question itself. For the past two years, the question has been "Can Intel survive?" After this financial report, that question has essentially been answered; the new question has become—how well can it survive, and how quickly?

data.title: US Soldier Charged After Using Secret Maduro Capture Operation Intel To Win Over $400,000 On Polymarket Prediction Bets
data.description:
Soldier Accused Of Turning Secret Military Operation Into Prediction Market Profits

A serving US Army soldier stationed at Fort Bragg has been charged with using classified military intelligence to place bets on a crypto prediction market tied to the removal of Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro, in what prosecutors describe as an unprecedented abuse of government secrets for personal gain.

Federal authorities allege Gannon Ken Van Dyke, 38, used inside knowledge from a covert operation to turn a $33,000 trading position into more than $409,000 in profit on Polymarket.

U.S. Soldier Charged With Using Classified Information To Profit From Prediction Market Bets

Gannon Ken Van Dyke allegedly made more than $400,000 trading on polymarket on the basis of classified information regarding the timing of a U.S. military operation to capture Nicolás… pic.twitter.com/528YaGQr8N

— U.S. Department of Justice (@TheJusticeDept) April 23, 2026

How Did $33,000 Turn Into Over $400,000 In Bets?

Court filings state Van Dyke placed 13 bets on Venezuela-related outcomes between 26 December 2025 and 2 January 2026.

His largest position reportedly involved a $32,537 wager that Maduro would be out of office by 31 January, which later delivered a return of over 1,200%, pushing total gains past $409,000.

Master Sgt. Gannon Ken Van Dyke helped plan the raid on Maduro.

Days before it went public, he bet $32,537 on Polymarket that Maduro would be out by Jan 31.

Profit: $404,222. Return: 1,242%.

The WOW: Polymarket runs on-chain. Every bet sits on a public Polygon ledger the…

— Vectorial Data (@VectorialData) April 24, 2026

Other trades included smaller positions predicting a US military presence in Venezuela and the likelihood of an invasion, all timed closely around sensitive developments linked to the operation.

According to investigators, the trading activity first surfaced publicly after the January events, when unusually accurate positioning in the market triggered scrutiny across prediction trading communities.

Was Operation Absolute Resolve Behind The Trades?

Prosecutors say the bets were linked to classified intelligence from “Operation Absolute Resolve”, a US military mission targeting Maduro.

Van Dyke is accused of accessing nonpublic information about the operation while serving in the armed forces, then using that knowledge to place trades ahead of the public announcement.

On 3 January 2026, US special forces reportedly carried out the operation in Caracas, detaining Maduro and his wife in the early hours.

The success of the mission was later confirmed publicly by the US President, triggering a sharp settlement of related prediction markets.

Authorities also claim Van Dyke was directly involved in aspects of the operation and had prior awareness of its timing and objectives.

🚨🇺🇸 U.S. Army soldier Gannon Ken Van Dyke has been charged with using classified intel on Venezuela to bet on Polymarket.

He helped plan "Operation Absolute Resolve," the op that captured Maduro on Jan 3, then bet $33K on "Maduro out" and on "U.S. forces in Venezuela"… pic.twitter.com/fgrWRmy89g

— International Cyber Digest (@IntCyberDigest) April 23, 2026

Why Did Suspicious Trading Raise Alarm?

The unusually well-timed bets quickly drew attention in prediction market circles, with traders questioning how such precise positioning could occur ahead of the announcement.

Within days of the operation being made public, Van Dyke allegedly attempted to delete his Polymarket account and misled the platform by claiming he had lost access to his email.

Investigators also say he tried to obscure links between his identity and the trading accounts, including changes to crypto exchange details and routing activity through a VPN.

The Maduro insider who made over $404,222 has been identified and arrested

DOJ arrested Gannon Ken Van Dyke today, a US special ops soldier

Just hours before "Operation Absolute Resolve" went public, he put $33,933 across four markets

When the media caught wind he tried to… pic.twitter.com/BrnjTGc2cw

— Polysights (@Polysights) April 24, 2026

Polymarket later referred the activity to US authorities, stating:

“Insider trading has no place on Polymarket. Today's arrest is proof the system works.”

What Charges Has The Soldier Faced?

Van Dyke now faces five federal charges, including:

  • Three counts under the Commodity Exchange Act
  • Wire fraud
  • Theft of nonpublic government information

Each commodities-related charge carries up to 10 years in prison, while the wire fraud count carries a maximum sentence of 20 years.

According to the DOJ indictment, Gannon Ken Van Dyke faces charges of:

- unlawful use of confidential government information for personal gain
- theft of nonpublic government information
- commodities fraud (3 counts under the Commodity Exchange Act)
- wire fraud
- unlawful…

— Grok (@grok) April 24, 2026

Federal prosecutors said the case demonstrates that existing insider trading laws apply even to decentralised prediction platforms, arguing that classified information cannot be used for financial speculation in any market format.

U.S. Attorney Jay Clayton said prediction markets are “not a haven for using misappropriated confidential or classified information for personal gain”, adding that those entrusted with national secrets must not exploit them for profit.

Are Prediction Markets Becoming A Legal Grey Zone?

The case has intensified scrutiny over prediction platforms such as Polymarket, which allow users to trade on real-world events using yes-or-no contracts.

Regulators, including the Commodity Futures Trading Commission, have raised concerns that such markets could be vulnerable to misuse by individuals with access to privileged or state-level information.

Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche said enforcement would evolve alongside the industry, noting that federal laws protecting national security information “fully apply” despite the relatively new nature of prediction markets.

CFTC just brought its first insider trading case involving prediction markets

Allegation: a U.S. service member used classified info about a Maduro operation to trade on Polymarket

This is a big deal:
-Event contracts are now clearly in scope
-Insider trading applications are… https://t.co/6UIqOlLjLi

— Nima Maleki (@Nimathefish) April 24, 2026

Legal experts also suggest prosecutors may rely on long-standing commodities and fraud statutes to pursue similar cases, even without specific laws designed for crypto prediction platforms.

First Major Test Case For Crypto Prediction Trading Laws?

This is believed to be the first major US prosecution involving alleged insider trading on a prediction market using classified government intelligence.

Authorities say the case highlights growing risks as online betting platforms expand into geopolitical events, military actions and government decisions.

Van Dyke remains in custody pending further proceedings, with investigators continuing to examine whether additional individuals may have been involved or benefited from related trades.

data.title: Federal Reserve Chair nominee Warsh: Technology-driven deflation is a major risk
data.description:
Author: Anthony J. Pompliano, Founder and CEO of Professional Capital Management; Translated by Shaw Jinse Finance

Last April, the US President took to the podium in the White House Rose Garden, triggering a violent upheaval in the financial markets. He announced a comprehensive tariff increase on all US imports, causing the stock market to plummet, academics to panic, and the internet to be filled with various predictions of an impending Great Depression.

Of course, this intense reaction was purely unwarranted. The stock market subsequently rebounded, repeatedly hitting new historical highs. A host of "experts" made inaccurate predictions, while those investors who ignored the noise and made rational investments reaped substantial profits.

However, one viewpoint in the mainstream analysis at the time seemed utterly absurd to me: various "experts" kept claiming that tariff policies would fuel hyperinflation. In contrast, I repeatedly emphasized in April 2025 that deflation was a far more serious threat than inflation. In a tweet on April 10, 2025, I wrote: "All the herd-following financial professionals are telling you that tariffs will drive up inflation, but I want to make it clear that the greater risk is actually deflation." To this day, I still maintain this judgment. You don't need to believe me, and you don't need to listen to me elaborate further. Kevin Warsh, the next chairman of the Federal Reserve, recently stated clearly during an interview with CNBC that he believes technological advancements will lead to a structural decline in prices, and that deflation is a serious risk that the Federal Reserve must prepare in advance. Artificial intelligence will lower the cost of all goods and services. We may be ushering in the beginning of a new round of productivity boom, and the major trend of structural price declines may have just begun. These remarks were made publicly by the soon-to-be head of the Federal Reserve on a national television program. If you still cannot clearly predict that the Federal Reserve will continue to cut interest rates in the coming months or even years, then probably no information can convince you. Some might claim that Warsh is merely a puppet of Trump, well aware that he must cut interest rates to avoid conflict with the president. Others argue that short-term increases in oil prices and long-term increases in commodity prices will push up inflation, thus limiting the Federal Reserve's room for significant interest rate cuts. I have no way of knowing Warsh's political stance, nor how he will work with the president. Only time will tell if these doubts are true. However, regarding the claim of rising inflation, in my view, deflation still poses a far greater threat to the US economy than inflation—this is beyond doubt. Oil and gas prices are indeed rising, as are commodity prices. But the US economy today is more reliant on the technology industry than ever before. Crucially, technology is significantly depressing the prices of various goods and services in the US. The structural decline in prices mentioned by Walsh has long been misunderstood by the public. As Jeff Booth once pointed out, "The natural state of a free market is deflation; prices will fall back to the marginal cost of production." I agree with this view. If this theory holds true, with the accelerated popularization of artificial intelligence and robotics, a continued decline in prices will become inevitable. Those who hold a rational opposing view will immediately point out that the US government's continuous money printing, the ever-growing national debt, and unrestrained fiscal spending have almost always triggered inflation historically. I do not deny that past experience has indeed confirmed this pattern. However, the current situation is unique in that a powerful deflationary force is dominating the US economy. Tariff policies, immigration deportations, artificial intelligence, and automation technologies—multiple factors combined—have formed a highly impactful deflationary force. As Elon Musk said, even if the US prints unlimited amounts of money, it will be difficult to offset this strong deflationary momentum. This argument may sound absurd and even seem jarring. But the more I delve into the data, the more convinced I become that deflation is the greater risk at present. The first major test for this view was last year's tariff policy. At that time, the market generally predicted a surge in inflation; however, deflationary forces directly suppressed inflation, and the price spike never materialized. The second crucial test will arrive in the next two to three months. Most investors expect inflation to rebound sharply, with the Consumer Price Index (CPI) rising by more than 5% year-on-year; however, real-time inflation indicators such as Truflation suggest that the CPI will likely remain moderate. If the CPI does not surge, astute market participants will see that deflationary forces are profoundly impacting the overall economy. Finally, and ultimately, the test: will there be a substantial decline in consumer prices? A halt to price increases is far from enough; it only alleviates cost pressures and cannot create a true deflationary dividend or reduce the burden of living. Price stabilization merely means that the cost of living has stopped worsening. However, once the prices of consumer goods for ordinary people fall across the board, the US economy may enter a golden age of development, entering an ideal environment of high growth and low inflation. This is precisely the economic scenario that central bank governors around the world dream of. All Americans should expect that artificial intelligence companies will bring a strong deflationary effect, boosting GDP while lowering the cost of living. Once this vision is realized, all politicians, central bank officials, and regulatory agencies will be seen as heroes of the era. Who claims credit for this economic victory is not important; the American people simply want lower prices to embrace economic prosperity. May we eventually achieve our goal.
